author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm XI

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To the end, a psalm of David himself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This title does not require a fresh consideration: for the meaning of, “to the end,” has already been sufficiently handled.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us then look to the text itself of the Psalm, which to me appears to be sung against the heretics, who, by rehearsing and exaggerating the sins of many in the Church, as if either all or the majority among themselves were righteous, strive to turn and snatch us away from the breasts of the one True Mother Church: affirming that Christ is with them, and warning us as if with piety and earnestness, that by passing over to them we may go over to Christ, whom they falsely declare they have.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it is known that in prophecy Christ, among the many names in which notice of Him is conveyed in allegory, is also called a mountain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We must accordingly answer these people, and say, “I trust in the Lord: how say ye to my soul, Remove into the mountains as a sparrow?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I keep to one mountain wherein I trust, how say ye that I should pass over to you, as if there were many Christs?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or if through pride you say that you are mountains, I had indeed need to be a sparrow winged with the powers and commandments of God: but these very things hinder my flying to these mountains, and placing my trust in proud men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have a house where I may rest, in that I trust in the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For even “the sparrow hath found her a house,” and, “The Lord hath become a refuge to the poor.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us say then with all confidence, lest while we seek Christ among heretics we lose Him, “In the Lord I trust: how say ye to my soul, Remove into the mountains as a sparrow?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For, lo, sinners have bent the bow, they have prepared their arrows in the quiver, that they may in the obscure moon shoot at the upright in heart” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These be the terrors of those who threaten us as touching sinners, that we may pass over to them as the righteous.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Lo,” they say, “the sinners have bent the bow:” the Scriptures, I suppose, by carnal interpretation of which they emit envenomed sentences from them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They have prepared their arrows in the quiver:” the same words, that is, which they will shoot out on the authority of Scripture, they have prepared in the secret place of the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “That they may in the obscure moon shoot at the upright in heart:” that when they see, from the Church’s light being obscured by the multitude of the unlearned and the carnal, that they cannot be convicted, they may corrupt good manners by evil communications.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But against all these terrors we must say, “In the Lord I trust.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now I remember that I promised to consider in this Psalm with what suitableness the moon signifies the Church.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There are two probable opinions concerning the moon: but of these which is the true, I suppose it either impossible or very difficult for a man to decide.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when we ask whence the moon has her light, some say that it is her own, but that of her globe half is bright, and half dark: and when she revolves in her own orbit, that part wherein she is bright gradually turns towards the earth, so as that it may be seen by us; and that therefore at first her appearance is as if she were horned.…According to this opinion the moon in allegory signifies the Church, because in its spiritual part the Church is bright, but in its carnal part is dark: and sometimes the spiritual part is seen by good works, but sometimes it lies hid in the conscience, and is known to God alone, since in the body alone is it seen by men.…But according to the other opinion also the moon is understood to be the Church, because she has no light of her own, but is lighted by the only-begotten Son of God, who in many places of holy Scripture is allegorically called the Sun.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whom certain heretics being ignorant of, and not able to discern Him, endeavour to turn away the minds of the simple to this corporeal and visible sun, which is the common light of the flesh of men and flies, and some they do pervert, who as long as they cannot behold with the mind the inner light of truth, will not be content with the simple Catholic faith; which is the only safety to babes, and by which milk alone they can arrive in assured strength at the firm support of more solid food.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whichever then of these two opinions be the true, the moon in allegory is fitly understood as the Church.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or if in such difficulties as these, troublesome rather than edifying, there be either no satisfaction or no leisure to exercise the mind, or if the mind itself be not capable of it, it is sufficient to regard the moon with ordinary eyes, and not to seek out obscure causes, but with all men to perceive her increasings and fulnesses and wanings; and if she wanes to the end that she may be renewed, even to this rude multitude she sets forth the image of the Church, in which the resurrection of the dead is believed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next we must enquire, what in this Psalm is meant by “the obscure moon,” in which sinners have prepared to shoot at the upright in heart?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For not in one way only may the moon be said to be obscure: for when her monthly course is finished, and when her brightness is interrupted by a cloud, and when she is eclipsed at the full, the moon may be called obscure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may then be understood first of the persecutors of the Martyrs, for that they wished in the obscure moon to shoot at the upright in heart; whether it be yet in the time of the Church’s youth, because she had not yet shone forth in greatness on the earth, and conquered the darkness of heathen superstitions; or by the tongues of blasphemers and such as defame the Christian name, when the earth was as it were beclouded, the moon, that is, the Church, could not be clearly seen; or when by the slaughter of the Martyrs themselves and so great effusion of blood, as by that eclipse and obscuration, wherein the moon seems to exhibit a bloody face, the weak were deterred from the Christian name; in which terror sinners shot out words crafty and sacrilegious to pervert even the upright in heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And secondly, it can be understood of these sinners, whom the Church contains, because at that time, taking the opportunity of this moon’s obscurity, they committed many crimes, which are now tauntingly objected to us by the heretics, whereas their founders are said to have been guilty of them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But howsoever that be which was done in the obscure moon, now that the Catholic name is spread and celebrated throughout the whole world, what concern of mine is it to be disturbed by things unknown?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “in the Lord I trust;” nor do I listen to them that say to my soul, “Remove into the mountains as a sparrow.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, lo, sinners have bent the bow, that they may in the obscure moon shoot at the upright in heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or if the moon seem even now obscure to them, because they would make it uncertain which is the Catholic Church, and they strive to convict her by the sins of those many carnal men whom she contains; what concern is this to him, who says in truth, “In the Lord I trust”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By which word every one shows that he is himself wheat, and endures the chaff with patience unto the time of winnowing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In the Lord,” therefore, “I trust.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let them fear who trust in man, and cannot deny that they are of man’s party, by whose grey hairs they swear; and when in conversation it is demanded of them, of what communion they are, unless they say that they are of his party, they cannot be recognised.…Or perhaps you will say that it is written, “Ye shall know them by their works”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I see indeed marvellous works the daily violences of the Circumcelliones, with the bishops and presbyters for their leaders, flying about in every direction, and calling their terrible clubs “Israels;” which men now living daily see and feel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But for the times of Macarius, respecting which they raise an invidious cry, most men have not seen them, and no one sees them now: and any Catholic who saw them could say, if he wished to be a servant of God, “In the Lord I trust.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let the Catholic soul then say, “In the Lord I trust; how say ye to my soul, Remove into the mountains as a sparrow?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, lo, the sinners have bent the bow, they have prepared their arrows in the quiver, that they may in the obscure moon shoot at the upright in heart:” and from them let her turn her speech to the Lord and say, “For they have destroyed what Thou hast perfected” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this let her say not against these only, but against all heretics.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they have all, as far as in them lies, destroyed the praise which God hath perfected out of the mouth of babes and sucklings, when they disturb the little ones with vain and scrupulous questions, and suffer them not to be nourished with the milk of faith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if then it were said to this soul, why do they say to you, “Remove into the mountains as a sparrow;” why do they frighten you with sinners, who “have bent the bow, to shoot in the obscure moon at the upright in heart”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: She answers, Therefore it is they frighten me, “because they have destroyed what Thou hast perfected.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where but in their conventicles, where they nourish not with milk, but kill with poison the babes and ignorant of the interior light.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But what hath the Just done?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If Macarius, if Cæcilianus, offend you, what hath Christ done to you, who said, “My peace I give unto you, My peace I leave with you;” which ye with your abominable dissensions have violated?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What hath Christ done to you? who with such exceeding patience endured His betrayer, as to give to him, as to the other Apostles, the first Eucharist consecrated with His own hands, and blessed with His own mouth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What hath Christ done to you? who sent this same betrayer, whom He called a devil, who before betraying the Lord could not show good faith even to the Lord’s purse, with the other disciples to preach the kingdom of heaven; that He might show that the gifts of God come to those that with faith receive them, though he, through whom they receive them, be such as Judas was.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord is in His holy temple” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 4), yea in such wise as the Apostle saith, “For the temple of God is holy, which” temple “ye are.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Now if any man shall violate the temple of God, him shall God destroy.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He violateth the temple of God, who violateth unity: for he “holdeth not the head, from which the whole body fitly joined together and compacted by that which every joint supplieth according to the working after the measure of every part maketh increase of the body to the edifying of itself in love.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Lord is in this His holy temple; which consisteth of His many members, fulfilling each his own separate duties, by love built up into one building.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which temple he violateth, who for the sake of his own pre-eminence separateth himself from the Catholic society.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord is in His holy temple; the Lord, His seat is in heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If you take heaven to be the just man, as you take the earth to be the sinner, to whom it was said, “Earth thou art, and unto earth shalt thou go;” the words, “The Lord is in His holy temple” you will understand to be repeated, whilst it is said, “The Lord, His seat is in heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His eyes look upon the poor.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His to Whom the poor man hath been left, and Who hath been made a refuge to the poor.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And therefore all the seditions and tumults within these nets, until they be drawn to shore, concerning which heretics upbraid us to their own ruin and our correction, are caused by those men, who will not be Christ’s poor.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But do they turn away God’s eyes from such as would be so?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For His eyes look upon the poor.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it to be feared lest, in the crowd of the rich, He may not be able to see the few poor, whom He brings up in safe keeping in the bosom of the Catholic Church?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His eyelids question the sons of men.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here by that rule I would wish to take “the sons of men” of those that from old men have been regenerated by faith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For these, by certain obscure passages of Scripture, as it were the closed eyes of God, are exercised that they may seek: and again, by certain clear passages, as it were the open eyes of God, are enlightened that they may rejoice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this frequent closing and opening in the holy Books are as it were the eyelids of God; which question, that is, which try the “sons of men;” who are neither wearied with the obscurity of the matter, but exercised; nor puffed up by knowledge, but confirmed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord questioneth the righteous and ungodly” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why then do we fear lest the ungodly should be any hurt to us, if so be they do with insincere heart share the sacraments with us, seeing that He “questioneth the righteous and the ungodly.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But whoso loveth iniquity, hateth his own soul:” that is, not him who believeth God, and putteth not his hope in man, but only his own soul doth the lover of iniquity hurt.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He shall rain snares upon the sinners” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If by clouds are understood prophets generally, whether good or bad, who are also called false prophets: false prophets are so ordered by the Lord God, that by them He may rain snares upon sinners.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For no one, but the sinner, falls into a following of them, whether by way of preparation for the last punishment, if he shall choose to persevere in sin; or to dissuade from pride, if in time he shall come to seek God with a more sincere intent.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if by clouds are understood good and true prophets only; by these too it is clear that God raineth snares upon sinners, although by them He watereth also the godly unto fruitfulness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “To some,” saith the Apostle, “we are the savour of life unto life; to some the savour of death unto death.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For not prophets only, but all who with the word of God water souls, may be called clouds.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who when they are understood amiss, God raineth snares upon sinners; but when they are understood aright, He maketh the hearts of the godly and believing fruitful.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As, for instance, the passage, “and they two shall be in one flesh,” if one interpret it with an eye to lust, He raineth a snare upon the sinner.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if you understand it, as he who says, “But I speak concerning Christ and the Church,” He raineth a shower on the fertile soil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now both are effected by the same cloud, that is, holy Scripture.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again the Lord says, “Not that which goeth into your mouth defileth you, but that which cometh out.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The sinner hears this, and makes ready his palate for gluttony: the righteous hears it, and is guarded against the superstitious distinction in meats.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here then also out of the same cloud of Scripture, according to the several desert of each, upon the sinner the rain of snares, upon the righteous the rain of fruitfulness, is poured.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Fire and brimstone and the blast of the tempest is the portion of their cup.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is their punishment and end, by whom the name of God is blasphemed; that first they should be wasted by the fire of their own lusts, then by the ill savour of their evil deeds cast off from the company of the blessed, at last carried away and overwhelmed suffer penalties unspeakable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is the portion of their cup: as of the righteous, “Thy cup inebriating how excellent is it! for they shall be inebriated with the richness of Thine house.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now I suppose a cup is mentioned for this reason, that we should not suppose that anything is done by God’s providence, even in the very punishments of sinners, beyond moderation and measure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And therefore as if he were giving a reason why this should be, he added, “For the Lord is righteous, and hath loved righteousnesses” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The plural not without meaning, but only because he speaks of men, is as that righteousnesses be understood to be used for righteous men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in many righteous men there seem, so to say, to be righteousnesses, whereas there is one only righteousness of God whereof they all participate.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Like as when one face looks upon many mirrors, what in it is one only, is by those many mirrors reflected manifoldly.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore he recurs to the singular, saying, “His face hath seen equity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perhaps, “His face hath seen equity,” is as if it were said, Equity hath been seen in His face, that is, in knowledge of Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For God’s face is the power by which He is made known to them that are worthy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or at least, “His face hath seen equity,” because He doth not allow Himself to be known by the evil, but by the good; and this is equity.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if any one would understand the moon of the synagogue, let him refer the Psalm to the Lord’s passion, and of the Jews say, “For they have destroyed what Thou hast perfected;” and of the Lord Himself, “But what hath the Just done?” whom they accused as the destroyer of the Law: whose precepts, by their corrupt living, and by despising them, and by setting up their own, they had destroyed, so that the Lord Himself may speak as Man, as He is wont, saying, “In the Lord I trust; how say ye to my soul, Remove into the mountains as a sparrow?” by reason, that is, of the fear of those who desire to apprehend and crucify Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since the interpretation is not unreasonable of sinners wishing to “shoot at the upright in heart,” that is, those who believed in Christ, “in the obscure moon,” that is, the Synagogue filled with sinners.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To this too the words, “The Lord is in His holy temple; the Lord, His seat is in heaven,” are suitable; that is, the Word in Man, or the very Son of Man who is in heaven.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His eyes look upon the poor;” either on to Him whom He assumed as God, or for whom He suffered as Man.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His eyelids question the sons of men.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The closing and opening of the eyes, which is probably meant by the word eyelids, we may take to be His death and resurrection, whereby He tried the sons of men His disciples, terrified at His passion, and gladdened by the resurrection.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord questioneth the righteous and ungodly,” even now from out of Heaven governing the Church.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But whoso loveth iniquity, hateth his own soul.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why it is so, what follows teaches us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “He shall rain snares upon the sinners:” which is to be taken according to the exposition above given, and so on with all the rest to the end of the Psalm.
